issues.description: Audit log pagination is performed in-memory after fetching all records from both tables without a database-level LIMIT. For large datasets, this loads the entire audit log into memory before slicing, which is both a performance risk and a potential DoS vector if the tables grow large.
issues.suggestion: Apply database-level pagination (skip/take) and sorting directly in the Prisma queries rather than fetching all records and sorting/slicing in JavaScript. Use separate paginated queries or a UNION approach at the database level.

issues.description: The audit log query for administrator logs does not scope results to the authenticated administrator's own records when the caller is a regular administrator. The controller spec says regular administrators should only see their own audit logs, but the provider applies no such filter — it queries all administrator audit logs regardless of the caller's identity.
issues.suggestion: Check the administrator's grade from the payload. If the caller is a regular administrator (not super), add a filter: `adminWhere.e_commerce_mall_administrator_id = props.administrator.id` to restrict results to their own audit entries.

issues.description: The seller approval requests query does not scope results to the authenticated seller's own requests. The function accepts a `SellerPayload` but never uses `props.seller.id` in the WHERE clause, meaning any authenticated seller could potentially view all sellers' approval requests.
issues.suggestion: Add `e_commerce_mall_seller_id: props.seller.id` to the `where` clause to ensure sellers can only see their own approval requests, unless the intent is for administrators to use this endpoint (in which case the auth decorator should be changed).

issues.description: All controllers use `console.log(error)` for error handling, which may expose stack traces and internal details to logs without structured context. This is the AutoBE standard pattern but lacks structured logging with request context, user identity, and severity levels.
issues.suggestion: Consider adopting a structured logging library (e.g., Winston, Pino) with request correlation IDs and sanitized error messages. Avoid logging full error objects that may contain sensitive data.

issues.description: The `orderCode` path parameter is typed as plain `string` without any format constraint (unlike other endpoints that use `tags.Format<'uuid'>`). This allows arbitrary string values including very long strings or special characters to reach the provider layer.
issues.suggestion: Add appropriate validation constraints to `orderCode` such as a maximum length tag (e.g., `string & tags.MaxLength<64>`) or a pattern constraint to limit the input surface.

issues.description: The default page limit is set to 100 items per page, which is unusually high and could result in large data transfers. Combined with no rate limiting, this could be used to enumerate product data efficiently.
issues.suggestion: Consider reducing the default limit to 20 and enforcing a maximum limit (e.g., 50 or 100) with explicit validation to prevent clients from requesting excessively large pages.

issues.description: Review snapshot is created before the review update in a non-atomic sequence. If the update fails after the snapshot is created, the database will contain an orphaned snapshot record with stale data, leading to data integrity issues.
issues.suggestion: Wrap the snapshot creation and review update in a single Prisma transaction ($transaction) to ensure atomicity, similar to the pattern used in putECommerceMallSellerRefundRequestsRefundRequestId.ts.

issues.description: The search filter uses `contains` without sanitization on user-supplied `email` and `search` fields. While Prisma ORM parameterizes queries preventing SQL injection, the `contains` operator with no length limit could be used for ReDoS-style abuse or excessive database load with very long search strings.
issues.suggestion: Add input length validation (e.g., max 255 characters) on search/email fields before passing to Prisma queries. Consider using typia validation tags on the IRequest interface.

issues.description: All matching variants are fetched into memory before pagination is applied (in-memory filtering and sorting). For products with large numbers of variants, this could cause excessive memory consumption and denial-of-service conditions. The `limit` parameter has no maximum cap, allowing a caller to request unlimited records.
issues.suggestion: Apply database-level filtering, sorting, and pagination where possible. Add a maximum limit cap (e.g., limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 20, 100)) to prevent unbounded queries.

issues.description: The duplicate review check queries by customer_id, product_id, and order_id but does NOT include order_item_id in the uniqueness constraint. A customer could potentially submit multiple reviews for different order items of the same product within the same order if the business logic intends one review per order item.
issues.suggestion: Clarify the intended uniqueness constraint. If one review per order item is intended, add `e_commerce_mall_order_item_id: orderItem.id` to the duplicate check query and enforce a unique database constraint on that column.

issues.description: Financial state-changing operations (refund approval/rejection, inventory restoration, order status transitions) lack audit logging. There is no record of who performed the action, when, or what the previous state was beyond the snapshot.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit log entries for financial operations including actor ID, action type, resource ID, previous state, and new state. Consider a dedicated audit_logs table or structured logging to an external system.

issues.description: No rate limiting on admin registration requests. A seller could spam registration requests (after each rejection) without restriction.
issues.suggestion: Implement rate limiting at the API gateway or middleware level for this endpoint. Consider also adding a cooldown period after rejection before allowing a new request.

issues.description: No upper bound on the 'limit' parameter. A client can request an arbitrarily large number of records (e.g., limit=1000000), causing excessive database load and potential DoS.
issues.suggestion: Cap the limit to a reasonable maximum, e.g., const limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 100, 500);

issues.description: When specific orderItemIds are provided, the code fetches those items without initially filtering by order ID. Although a cross-order check is done afterwards in the classification loop, items from other orders are still fetched from the database, leaking their existence. The ownership check should be applied at the query level.
issues.suggestion: Add 'e_commerce_mall_order_id: order.id' to the where clause of the specific-items query to enforce ownership at the database level: where: { id: { in: props.body.orderItemIds }, e_commerce_mall_order_id: order.id }

issues.description: The sort_field parameter is accepted from user input and mapped to an orderBy clause. While the current mapping is safe (only 'email' and 'created_at' are used), the pattern of accepting sort fields from user input should be validated against an explicit allowlist to prevent future injection if the mapping logic is changed.
issues.suggestion: Validate sort_field against an explicit allowlist: const allowedSortFields = ['email', 'created_at']; and reject unknown values with a 400 error.

issues.description: Force-refund is a sensitive administrative action but no audit log entry is created outside the order item status logs. The acting super administrator's identity and timestamp are not recorded in a dedicated audit trail.
issues.suggestion: Consider writing an audit log entry that records the super administrator ID, timestamp, order code, and list of refunded item IDs for compliance and forensic purposes.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/359 files] [3 chunks, 18→16 issues] The codebase follows AutoBE patterns with proper role-based authentication decorators (SellerAuth, CustomerAuth, AdministratorAuth, SuperadministratorAuth) applied consistently across all controllers. The main security concerns are: (1) a missing ownership scope filter in patchECommerceMallSellerApprovalRequests that could allow cross-seller data access, (2) missing role-based data scoping in the audit log provider for regular administrators, and (3) in-memory pagination of audit logs that fetches all records before filtering. The force-cancel endpoint's unvalidated string parameter is a minor concern. Standard AutoBE patterns like console.log error handling and missing rate limiting are noted as suggestions only.

items.message: Class "EcommercemallAuthAdministratorController" missing JSDoc

lines.text:
* Register a new administrator account on the platform.

Creates an administrator account using the provided email and password credentials. The email must be unique across all administrators — attempting to register with an already-registered email results in a conflict error. The password is securely hashed before storage.

Upon successful registration, the administrator is automatically authenticated: a JWT access token and refresh token are issued and returned in the response body. A session record is created in the e_commerce_mall_administrator_sessions table tracking the client IP address and request URL for audit purposes.

This endpoint is accessible to administrator actor context and allows initial account creation. After registration, the administrator can use the returned tokens for subsequent authenticated API requests.
